pullRequest.title: Generalize dice_loss with alpha/beta weighting
pullRequest.body:
This should now close: https://github.com/google-deepmind/optax/issues/1358

As per the discussion here: https://github.com/google-deepmind/optax/pull/1366#issuecomment-3218399437 the weighing with dice should accommodate some more overlap losses automatically.

pullRequest.title: Add a bias_correction_v flag to scale_by_amsgrad to align with the original AMSGrad paper and Pytorch/tensorflow impl
Fix conjugation in Newton-Schulz iterator and update tests for comple…
pullRequest.body:
Adds support for complex parameters in Muon, by adding .conj() to Newton-Schulz and the adaptive scaling step.

Also adds two tests. The first checks that the Newton-Schulz algorithm is able to make the updates orthogonal for real parameters and unitary for complex parameters. The second tests the Muon optimizer on a simple complex optimization problem.

Fixes #1494.

pullRequest.title: Clarify difference between kl_divergence and convex_kl_divergence
pullRequest.body:
# Description

This PR addresses issue #1513 by clarifying the documentation for `kl_divergence` and `convex_kl_divergence`.

The previous documentation did not explicitly state the mathematical difference between the two implementations, nor did it explain the specific use case for the convex variant.

**Changes made:**
- Updated `kl_divergence` docstring to identify it as the standard definition.
- Updated `convex_kl_divergence` docstring to:
  - Explicitly mention the added term: `sum(exp(log_predictions)) - sum(targets)`.
  - Clarify that this is the "Generalized KL Divergence" intended for unnormalized distributions.
  - Improved phrasing for readability and elegance.

Fixes #1513

## Type of change

- [x] Documentation update (no code logic change)

pullRequest.title: Add madgrad optimizer
pullRequest.body:
## Description

Closes #1582

This PR implements the MADGRAD optimizer in optax.contrib, porting the mathematical operations from the official PyTorch implementation to JAX.



MADGRAD is a general-purpose "Dual Averaging" optimizer that aims to bridge the gap between adaptive methods (like Adam) and stochastic gradient descent (SGD). It is designed to match the fast convergence of adaptive methods on NLP tasks while maintaining the superior generalization of SGD on vision tasks.



## References

- **Paper:** [Adaptivity without Compromise: A Momentumized, Adaptive, Dual Averaged Gradient Method for Stochastic Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2101.11075) (Defazio & Jelassi, 2021)

- **Source:** Ported faithfully from the [official PyTorch implementation](https://github.com/facebookresearch/madgrad).



## Technical Overview

MADGRAD computes updates using a weighted sum of gradients relative to the *initial* parameters ($x_0$), rather than just the previous step. The implementation follows the Dual Averaging formulation:



$$\lambda_k = \text{lr} \cdot \sqrt{k+1}$$

> **Step Size Scaling:** The effective step size scales up with the square root of the iteration count ($k$), ensuring older gradients don't vanish too quickly.



$$s_{k+1} = s_k + \lambda_k \cdot g_k$$

> **Gradient Accumulator:** Tracks the weighted sum of all past gradients (conceptually similar to the "velocity" or momentum vector).



$$G_{k+1} = G_k + \lambda_k \cdot g_k^2$$

> **Adaptive Normalizer:** Tracks the weighted sum of squared gradients to normalize updates, preventing the optimizer from getting stuck in steep regions (similar to Adagrad).



$$z_{k+1} = x_0 - \frac{s_{k+1}}{(G_{k+1})^{1/3} + \epsilon}$$

> **Dual Update:** Computes the new "target" parameter $z$ by applying the accumulated gradient $s$ (normalized by the cube root of $G$) directly to the initial weights $x_0$.



$$x_{k+1} = (1 - \text{momentum}) z_{k+1} + \text{momentum} \cdot x_k$$

> **Final Update:** The actual parameter value is a momentum-weighted blend of the new target $z$ and the previous parameter $x$.
